About

The person
behind the programs

I went to UCF for film and advertising, which taught me more about audiences than any communications textbook would have. Most of what stuck was the idea that attention is not given, it's earned, and the way you lose it is by treating people like passive recipients of whatever you need to say.

My first real marketing role was at Gallagher Promotional Products — handling email campaigns, social content, website maintenance, copywriting, and the company's Amazon storefront. When COVID forced difficult business decisions in 2022, Brad Carlson still put his name on a formal recommendation letter. I've since returned to Gallagher in a consulting capacity, which is the most honest measure of how that relationship held. I also turned the platform work into something shareable: a practical guide to selling on Amazon for small businesses, published on LinkedIn for anyone navigating the same terrain.

The golf industry came next. At Golfzon Leadbetter Academy, I managed communications across 30+ international franchise locations. That scale forced me to stop thinking in individual emails and start thinking in systems — what goes out, to whom, in what order, reviewed by whom, measured how. That shift is probably the most durable thing I took from that period.

One of my most complete independent engagements has been with Exuma Fragrance Co., an indie perfume house out of Winter Park. I built the brand identity from scratch — visual system, photography direction, web presence, and Etsy shop — for a catalog that has grown to more than 70 handcrafted fragrances with a global customer base and 2,600+ Instagram followers. Brand work that you can point to and say: that's what it looked like before, and that's what it looks like now.

Most messaging problems aren't writing problems. They're workflow problems, governance problems, or audience problems in disguise. When the infrastructure is right, the writing comes easier and the results hold longer.

UCF ’20HubSpotSalesforce WordPress / WooCommerceGoogle Ads Certified Adobe SuiteMailchimp / KlaviyoAsana / Monday

What I Do

Four disciplines.
One person who does all of them.

01
Internal Communications Programs

Editorial calendar, cadence model, distribution workflow, approval process, audience segmentation, and the analytics review that makes the next cycle better than the last. Not just writing — the system that produces it.

Editorial CalendarContent GovernanceHubSpotEmployee Engagement
02
Executive & Leadership Messaging

Translating what leadership knows into what people can act on. Announcements, change communications, and sensitive disclosures handled with appropriate sequencing and audience targeting. Clarity without backlash.

Executive BriefsTalking PointsChange CommsTone Calibration
03
Email & Lifecycle Campaign Management

Strategy, copy, CRM workflow architecture (HubSpot, Klaviyo, Mailchimp), list hygiene, send-cadence logic, and the monthly engagement review that changes what gets sent next. End-to-end management.

Campaign StrategyCopywritingSegmentationAnalytics
04
Brand Identity & Content Systems

Full brand builds from scratch (Exuma Fragrance Co.) to multi-location voice standardization (Golfzon, 30+ markets). Visual identity, voice and tone guidelines, and the template architecture that keeps it consistent.

Brand IdentityVoice & ToneTemplate SystemsMulti-Location

Writing

Ideas worth
sending

Request writing samples →
The unread internal email and what it's actually telling you
Strategy · Internal Communications

The Unread Internal Email and What It's Actually Telling You

A 12% open rate on an internal newsletter isn't an indictment of the writing. It's a symptom. Most internal communications problems trace back to cadence, audience definition, or the approval process that made the thing late. The email is the last 10% of the problem.

7 min · Internal Communications
What I learned building for 30 locations
Field Notes · Case Study

What Coordinating Across 30 Locations Actually Taught Me

The lesson wasn't about scale or templates. It was about the difference between a framework people follow and one they quietly ignore. Those two outcomes look identical for the first two months.

5 min · Operations
On open rates and what they don't mean
Analytics · Email Strategy

Your Open Rate Went Up. Here's Why That Might Not Mean Anything.

A rising open rate on a lower send volume means you've started sending less often to the same people who were already paying attention. That's not growth. That's getting quieter and calling it success.

4 min · Analytics
